title: A Workshop on Public Speaking
summary: A workshop facilitator and a participant discuss practical techniques for overcoming nervousness and becoming a more confident public speaker.

body.speaker: Facilitator
body.text: Welcome, everyone. Before we begin the first exercise, I'd like to ask — how many of you feel genuinely comfortable speaking in front of a group?

body.speaker: Participant
body.text: I'll be honest — I find it terrifying. Even a small meeting at work makes me nervous.

body.speaker: Facilitator
body.text: That's completely normal. Research suggests that public speaking is one of the most common fears, even among experienced professionals.

body.speaker: Participant
body.text: So is it something you can actually learn, or is it just a personality thing?

body.speaker: Facilitator
body.text: Absolutely, it can be learned. Confidence in speaking is a skill, not a fixed personality trait. The brain responds well to gradual exposure and practice.

body.speaker: Participant
body.text: But when I stand up to speak, my mind goes blank. I forget everything I prepared.

body.speaker: Facilitator
body.text: That happens because anxiety activates the fight-or-flight response. Your body is ready to run, not to give a speech. Deep breathing before you speak can help reset that response.

body.speaker: Participant
body.text: I've heard that before, but it feels a bit awkward to stand there breathing when everyone is watching.

body.speaker: Facilitator
body.text: You don't have to do it publicly. Try three slow, deep breaths before you even stand up. Most people in the room won't notice.

body.speaker: Participant
body.text: What about preparation? I usually write out my whole speech word for word, but then I panic if I lose my place.

body.speaker: Facilitator
body.text: That's a very common trap. Reading a script word for word makes you sound robotic and increases your anxiety when you lose the thread. I recommend using short bullet points instead.

body.speaker: Participant
body.text: But won't I forget important points if I'm not reading from a script?

body.speaker: Facilitator
body.text: If you know your topic well, you won't need every word in front of you. Bullet points remind you of the structure, but your natural language fills in the details — and it actually sounds much more authentic.

body.speaker: Participant
body.text: That makes sense. What about eye contact? I always end up staring at the floor or the back wall.

body.speaker: Facilitator
body.text: Eye contact is one of the biggest factors in how an audience perceives your confidence. Try the triangle technique — move your gaze between three points in the room, briefly staying at each for two or three seconds.

body.speaker: Participant
body.text: That sounds manageable. Is there anything I can do in daily life to practise — not just in formal presentations?

body.speaker: Facilitator
body.text: Absolutely. Every conversation is an opportunity to practise. Start small — contribute one point in your next team meeting. Order your coffee by speaking clearly and making eye contact. Record yourself talking for sixty seconds about a topic you enjoy.

body.speaker: Participant
body.text: Recording myself is something I haven't tried. It sounds uncomfortable, but I can see why it would help.

body.speaker: Facilitator
body.text: It's uncomfortable at first, but most people are surprised. You often sound far better than you imagine. The voice in your head is harsher than the one other people hear.

body.speaker: Participant
body.text: I suppose my biggest fear is that I'll say something wrong and people will judge me.

body.speaker: Facilitator
body.text: Here's the thing — audiences are generally on your side. They want you to succeed. Most people in the room are too busy thinking about their own nervousness to judge yours.

annotations.meaningVi: thực sự thoải mái, không giả vờ
annotations.exampleEn: She was genuinely comfortable speaking to large audiences after years of practice.
He found the thought of presenting to the board terrifying.
Curiosity is a personality trait that helps people learn quickly.

annotations.meaningVi: tiếp xúc dần dần, tiếp cận từng bước
annotations.exampleEn: Gradual exposure to stressful situations helps reduce anxiety over time.

annotations.meaningVi: đầu óc tôi trống rỗng, quên hết
annotations.exampleEn: Every time someone asks me a question in a meeting, my mind goes blank.

annotations.meaningVi: phản ứng chiến đấu hoặc bỏ chạy (phản ứng căng thẳng của cơ thể)
annotations.exampleEn: The body's fight-or-flight response can make you feel anxious even in safe situations.
Deep breathing for a few minutes can calm the nervous system before a presentation.

annotations.meaningVi: từng từ một, y nguyên văn
annotations.exampleEn: He memorised the speech word for word, which made it sound unnatural.

annotations.meaningVi: mất mạch ý, quên đang nói đến đâu
annotations.exampleEn: If you read from a script, you lose the thread the moment you make a mistake.

annotations.meaningVi: gạch đầu dòng, các điểm tóm tắt
annotations.exampleEn: She prepared bullet points rather than a full script for her presentation.

annotations.meaningVi: chân thực, tự nhiên, không giả tạo
annotations.exampleEn: Speaking in your own words sounds much more authentic than reading from notes.
Eye contact affects how the audience perceives the speaker's confidence.

annotations.meaningVi: kỹ thuật tam giác (nhìn vào ba điểm khác nhau trong phòng)
annotations.exampleEn: Using the triangle technique helped her avoid staring at just one person while speaking.

annotations.meaningVi: có thể thực hiện được, không quá khó
annotations.exampleEn: Breaking a big task into small steps makes it feel more manageable.

annotations.meaningVi: đứng về phía bạn, ủng hộ bạn
annotations.exampleEn: Remember that most audiences are on your side — they want you to do well.

grammarNotes.title: Second Conditional — Imagining hypothetical situations
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Hội thoại dùng điều kiện loại 2 khi giả định tình huống giả định: 'Recording myself is something I haven't tried. It sounds uncomfortable, but I can see why it would help.' Cấu trúc: If + past simple, would + infinitive. Loại điều kiện này diễn tả điều gì đó mà người nói tưởng tượng hoặc coi là không có thật hiện tại. Ví dụ: 'If I used bullet points, I would sound more natural.'
grammarNotes.bodyEn: The dialogue uses second conditional thinking when imagining hypothetical scenarios: 'It sounds uncomfortable, but I can see why it would help.' Form: If + past simple, would + infinitive. This conditional describes something the speaker imagines or considers currently untrue. Example: 'If I used bullet points, I would sound more natural.'

grammarNotes.title: Questions with 'What about…?' and 'Is there anything…?' — Politely asking for more information
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Người tham gia dùng các cấu trúc lịch sự để hỏi thêm thông tin: 'What about preparation?', 'What about eye contact?', 'Is there anything I can do in daily life to practise?'. 'What about + noun/gerund?' là cách tự nhiên và lịch sự để chuyển sang chủ đề mới. 'Is there anything I can do to…?' dùng khi hỏi gợi ý hành động. Đây là các cấu trúc phổ biến trong hội thoại thực tế ở B1-B2.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: The participant uses polite structures to ask for more information: 'What about preparation?', 'What about eye contact?', 'Is there anything I can do in daily life to practise?' 'What about + noun/gerund?' is a natural and polite way to shift to a new topic. 'Is there anything I can do to…?' is used when asking for action suggestions. These are common structures in real conversation at B1-B2 level.

translationVi:
Người hướng dẫn: Chào mừng tất cả mọi người. Trước khi chúng ta bắt đầu bài tập đầu tiên, tôi muốn hỏi — có bao nhiêu người trong số các bạn cảm thấy thực sự thoải mái khi nói trước một nhóm người?

Người tham gia: Thật lòng mà nói — tôi thấy điều đó thật đáng sợ. Ngay cả một cuộc họp nhỏ ở nơi làm việc cũng khiến tôi lo lắng.

Người hướng dẫn: Điều đó hoàn toàn bình thường. Nghiên cứu cho thấy nói trước công chúng là một trong những nỗi sợ phổ biến nhất, ngay cả ở những chuyên gia có kinh nghiệm.

Người tham gia: Vậy đó có phải là điều thực sự có thể học được không, hay chỉ là vấn đề tính cách?

Người hướng dẫn: Hoàn toàn có thể học được. Sự tự tin khi nói là một kỹ năng, không phải đặc điểm tính cách cố định. Não bộ phản hồi tốt với việc tiếp xúc dần dần và luyện tập.

Người tham gia: Nhưng khi tôi đứng dậy để nói, đầu óc tôi trống rỗng. Tôi quên hết mọi thứ đã chuẩn bị.

Người hướng dẫn: Điều đó xảy ra vì lo lắng kích hoạt phản ứng chiến đấu hoặc bỏ chạy. Cơ thể bạn đang sẵn sàng chạy trốn, không phải để diễn thuyết. Hít thở sâu trước khi nói có thể giúp đặt lại phản ứng đó.

Người tham gia: Tôi đã nghe điều đó rồi, nhưng cảm thấy hơi kỳ cục khi đứng đó thở trong khi mọi người đang nhìn.

Người hướng dẫn: Bạn không cần phải làm điều đó trước công chúng. Hãy thử ba nhịp thở chậm và sâu trước khi bạn đứng dậy. Hầu hết mọi người trong phòng sẽ không để ý.

Người tham gia: Còn về việc chuẩn bị thì sao? Tôi thường viết ra toàn bộ bài phát biểu từng từ một, nhưng rồi hoảng sợ khi bị mất chỗ.

Người hướng dẫn: Đó là một cái bẫy rất phổ biến. Đọc theo kịch bản từng từ một khiến bạn nghe có vẻ như robot và tăng thêm lo lắng khi bạn mất mạch ý. Tôi khuyến nghị sử dụng các điểm gạch đầu dòng ngắn thay thế.

Người tham gia: Nhưng tôi sẽ không quên những điểm quan trọng nếu không đọc từ kịch bản sao?

Người hướng dẫn: Nếu bạn hiểu rõ chủ đề, bạn sẽ không cần từng chữ trước mặt. Gạch đầu dòng nhắc bạn về cấu trúc, còn ngôn ngữ tự nhiên của bạn sẽ lấp đầy các chi tiết — và thực ra nghe chân thực hơn nhiều.

Người tham gia: Nghe có lý đấy. Còn về giao tiếp bằng mắt thì sao? Tôi luôn nhìn chằm chằm xuống sàn hoặc tường phía sau.

Người hướng dẫn: Giao tiếp bằng mắt là một trong những yếu tố lớn nhất ảnh hưởng đến cách khán giả nhận thấy sự tự tin của bạn. Hãy thử kỹ thuật tam giác — di chuyển ánh mắt giữa ba điểm trong phòng, dừng lại ở mỗi điểm khoảng hai hoặc ba giây.

Người tham gia: Nghe có vẻ thực hiện được. Có điều gì tôi có thể làm trong cuộc sống hàng ngày để luyện tập không — không chỉ trong các buổi thuyết trình chính thức?

Người hướng dẫn: Hoàn toàn được. Mọi cuộc trò chuyện đều là cơ hội để luyện tập. Bắt đầu nhỏ — đóng góp một điểm trong cuộc họp nhóm tiếp theo của bạn. Gọi cà phê bằng cách nói rõ ràng và giao tiếp bằng mắt. Tự ghi âm mình nói trong sáu mươi giây về một chủ đề bạn thích.

Người tham gia: Tự ghi âm là điều tôi chưa thử. Nghe có vẻ không thoải mái, nhưng tôi có thể hiểu tại sao điều đó lại hữu ích.

Người hướng dẫn: Lúc đầu không thoải mái, nhưng hầu hết mọi người đều ngạc nhiên. Bạn thường nghe hay hơn những gì bạn tưởng tượng. Giọng nói trong đầu bạn khắt khe hơn giọng mà người khác nghe thấy.

Người tham gia: Tôi nghĩ nỗi sợ lớn nhất của tôi là sẽ nói điều gì đó sai và mọi người sẽ phán xét tôi.

Người hướng dẫn: Đây là điều này — khán giả thường đứng về phía bạn. Họ muốn bạn thành công. Hầu hết mọi người trong phòng đang bận nghĩ về sự lo lắng của chính họ đến mức không có thời gian để phán xét bạn.

questions.prompt: How does the participant describe her feelings about speaking in front of a group?
options: She says she finds it boring but manageable
She says she finds it terrifying, even in small work meetings
She says she is confident in small groups but not large ones
She says she used to be nervous but has improved recently
questions.explanation: The participant says 'I find it terrifying. Even a small meeting at work makes me nervous.'
questions.hint: The participant uses a strong word to describe her fear.

questions.prompt: What does the facilitator say about the fear of public speaking?
options: It is a sign of introversion that cannot be changed
It is unusual and only affects people without experience
It is one of the most common fears, even among experienced professionals
It disappears naturally as people get older
questions.explanation: The facilitator says 'public speaking is one of the most common fears, even among experienced professionals.'
questions.hint: The facilitator says this fear is common, not unusual.

questions.prompt: According to the facilitator, is confidence in speaking a fixed personality trait?
options: Yes, some people are naturally confident speakers and others are not
No, it is a skill that can be learned through gradual exposure and practice
It depends on factors like education and upbringing
Partially — personality plays a role but can be overcome with effort
questions.explanation: The facilitator says 'Confidence in speaking is a skill, not a fixed personality trait. The brain responds well to gradual exposure and practice.'
questions.hint: The facilitator directly answers the participant's question.

questions.prompt: Why does the participant's mind go blank when she stands up to speak?
options: Because she has not prepared enough material
Because anxiety activates the fight-or-flight response
Because she is not used to standing while speaking
Because the room is usually too bright or too loud
questions.explanation: The facilitator explains: 'anxiety activates the fight-or-flight response. Your body is ready to run, not to give a speech.'
questions.hint: The facilitator explains a biological mechanism.

questions.prompt: What does the facilitator recommend to reset the anxiety response?
options: Drinking a glass of water before speaking
Taking three slow, deep breaths before standing up
Reminding yourself that everyone is nervous
Arriving early to get used to the room
questions.explanation: The facilitator says 'Try three slow, deep breaths before you even stand up.'
questions.hint: The facilitator gives a specific, simple physical technique.

questions.prompt: What problem does the participant identify with writing out a speech word for word?
options: It takes too long to prepare
She panics if she loses her place
It makes the speech too long
The audience can tell she is reading
questions.explanation: The participant says 'I panic if I lose my place.'
questions.hint: The participant describes what happens when something goes wrong with the script.

questions.prompt: What does the facilitator recommend as an alternative to a full script?
options: Writing only an introduction and conclusion
Drawing a mind map of key topics
questions.explanation: The facilitator says 'I recommend using short bullet points instead.'
questions.hint: The facilitator names a specific type of note-taking.

questions.prompt: What is the triangle technique used for?
options: Organising the structure of a speech into three parts
Positioning your notes on the stage
Managing eye contact by looking at three different points in the room
A breathing exercise using three steps
questions.explanation: The facilitator describes it as: 'move your gaze between three points in the room, briefly staying at each for two or three seconds.'
questions.hint: The triangle technique involves the eyes, not the speech structure.

questions.prompt: What everyday practice does the facilitator NOT suggest for daily speaking practice?
options: Contributing a point in a team meeting
Ordering coffee while making eye contact
Recording yourself speaking for sixty seconds
questions.explanation: The facilitator suggests small daily activities. Joining a debate club is not mentioned.
questions.hint: The facilitator focuses on informal, everyday opportunities — not formal clubs.

questions.prompt: What does the facilitator say happens when most people record themselves speaking?
options: They are disappointed because they sound worse than expected
They are surprised to find they sound far better than they imagined
They immediately identify and fix their bad habits
They become more self-conscious and less confident
questions.explanation: The facilitator says 'most people are surprised. You often sound far better than you imagine.'
questions.hint: The facilitator's point is that our self-perception is more negative than reality.

questions.prompt: What reassurance does the facilitator give about audiences judging speakers?
options: Audiences always judge speakers but quickly forget mistakes
Most audiences are experts and appreciate good technique
Audiences are generally on your side and too busy with their own nervousness to judge you
It is impossible to avoid being judged, so you should not worry about it
questions.explanation: The facilitator says 'audiences are generally on your side' and 'Most people in the room are too busy thinking about their own nervousness to judge yours.'
questions.hint: The facilitator shifts the focus from the speaker's fear to the audience's perspective.

cloze.template: Facilitator: Before we begin, how many of you feel genuinely comfortable speaking in front of a group? Participant: I'll be honest — I find it {{b1}}. Even a small meeting at work makes me nervous. Facilitator: That's completely normal. Research suggests that public speaking is one of the most common fears, even among experienced {{b2}}. Participant: So is it something you can actually learn, or is it just a personality {{b3}}? Facilitator: Absolutely, it can be learned. Confidence in speaking is a skill, not a fixed personality trait. The brain responds well to {{b4}} exposure and practice. Participant: But when I stand up to speak, my mind goes {{b5}}. I forget everything I prepared. Facilitator: That happens because anxiety activates the fight-or-flight response. Deep breathing before you speak can help {{b6}} that response. Participant: What about preparation? I usually write out my whole speech {{b7}}, but then I panic if I lose my place. Facilitator: Reading a script word for word makes you sound {{b8}} and increases your anxiety when you lose the thread. I recommend using short bullet points instead. Facilitator: Bullet points remind you of the structure, but your natural language fills in the details — and it actually sounds much more {{b9}}. Participant: What about eye contact? I always end up staring at the floor. Facilitator: Eye contact is one of the biggest factors in how an audience {{b10}} your confidence. Try the triangle technique — move your gaze between three points in the room. Facilitator: Audiences are generally on your side. Most people in the room are too busy thinking about their own nervousness to {{b11}} yours.

blanks.explanation: 'I find it terrifying' — 'terrifying' is the strongest word used, showing extreme fear of public speaking.
'Experienced professionals' — this phrase emphasises that even skilled, experienced people share this fear.
The participant uses 'personality thing' colloquially, which the facilitator then clarifies as 'personality trait' — showing both informal and formal usage.
'Gradual exposure' means increasing contact with the feared situation step by step — a key concept in managing anxiety.
'My mind goes blank' is a fixed idiom meaning you suddenly cannot remember or think of anything.
'Help reset that response' — 'reset' means to return to a neutral or starting state, as if restarting a system.
'Write out my speech word for word' — this fixed phrase means to write or record something exactly as it appears, without changing anything.
'Sound robotic' means to speak in a flat, mechanical way without natural expression — a common problem when reading directly from a script.
'Sounds much more authentic' — 'authentic' describes speech that feels genuine and real rather than rehearsed or artificial.
'How an audience perceives your confidence' — 'perceives' means understands or interprets through their senses and impressions.
'Too busy thinking about their own nervousness to judge yours' — 'judge' means to form a critical opinion of someone.

criticalThinkingQuestion: The facilitator suggests that the fear of public speaking comes partly from worrying too much about what others think. Do you think this is the main reason people are afraid of speaking in public? Are there other factors that might be equally or more important?
